citation.title: Machine learning for the advancement of membrane science and technology: A critical review

content:
A B S T R A C T Machine learning (ML) has been rapidly transforming the landscape of natural sciences and has the potentialto revolutionize the process of data analysis and hypothesis formulation as well as expand scientific knowledge. ML has been particularly instrumental in the advancement of cheminformatics and materials science,including membrane technology. In this review, we analyze the current state-of-the-art membrane-related MLapplications from ML and membrane perspectives. We first discuss the ML foundations of different algorithmsand design choices. Then, traditional and deep learning methods, including application examples from themembrane literature, are reported. We also discuss the importance of learning data and both molecular andmembrane-system featurization. Moreover, we follow up on the discussion with examples of ML applications inmembrane science and technology. We detail the literature using data-driven methods from property predictionto membrane fabrication. Various fields are also discussed, such as reverse osmosis, gas separation, andnanofiltration. We also differentiate between downstream predictive tasks and generative membrane design.Additionally, we formulate best practices and the minimum requirements for reporting reproducible ML studiesin the field of membranes. This is the first systematic and comprehensive review of ML in membrane science.

1. Introduction Data-driven approaches such as machine learning (ML) and artificial intelligence have undergone transformative changes in the lastdecade, considerably impacting science and technology [1]. From pioneering advancements in image recognition [2], deep neural networks(DNNs) [3], and sophisticated language models [4], ML has revolutionized various scientific fields. The discovery potential of ML algorithmsto identify new patterns in complex multidimensional data has resultedin advancements such as protein structure prediction tools includingDeepMind AlphaFold [5], accelerated drug discovery [6] and material discovery with GNoME [7] or introduced joint multidomain pretraining(JMP) [8]. Several of these multidimensional challenges associated with membranes have also been reported, including the development of novelpolymer materials, membrane process optimization, and system design and integration. Nonlinear approaches with multiple competingparameters are required to address these issues, which can be resourceintensive. Moreover, environmental and societal issues necessitate thedevelopment of an appropriate and effective solution. Most challenges in membrane science are high-dimensional: themembrane, solutes or gases, the solvents and the different processparameters are all influencing the properties of the final operations. Learning from data Scientific exploration and research has traditionally followed a common pattern: novel information and scientific advancements have usually depended on formulating hypotheses and the theoretical conceptualization of physical rules.

citation.title: Machine learning for the advancement of membrane science and technology: A critical review

content: novel information and scientific advancements have usually depended on formulating hypotheses and the theoretical conceptualization of physical rules. These hypotheses and theories are basedon observation or deductions from previously validated theories andare proven via experimentation. Although the rules and standards ofscientific experimentation and confirmation of hypotheses have developed over time, a theory is usually considered de facto correct if anexperiment or empirical evidence does not contradict it.In the context of natural sciences and engineering, this process canbe described as model formulation and validation. A model of thephysical world is developed based on initial observations, and additional experiments are performed to test whether the developed modelprovides good description or not. The model is iteratively updatedbased on the empirical evidence generated to support or contradict it.In other words, as far as hypothesis-driven approaches are concerned, experiments are conducted to support a preexisting hypothetical model,which is fine-tuned in an iterative manner.However, with the advent of data science [18], researchers haveadopted a new philosophy of scientific research, as shown in Fig. 2. Theexponential increase in computational power and available data haspaved ways toward ML techniques [19,20].

content: scientific research, as shown in Fig. 2. Theexponential increase in computational power and available data haspaved ways toward ML techniques [19,20]. The idea behind ML is thatif sufficient data are available, physical phenomena can be modeledwithout a priori conceptualization of a physical rule, almost withoutexception at the cost of interpretability. Data can be used to trainhighly adaptable ML models, which are mathematical structures thatcan capture the patterns recognized in data. Model training involvestuning the model parameters based on a target objective. After training,the model can be validated to ensure that it accurately describesthe examined phenomenon. The enhancement in mathematical modelperformance based on available information is known as ML (Fig. 3).As little to no a priori conceptualization of the model is involved inML, the performance of the final model heavily relies on the amountand quality of data used for its training [21]. Data generated via experiments or using data mining techniques is crucial and fundamental forefficient ML. The accuracy of a trained ML model is then confirmed viavalidation and testing, during which the model performs predictions onpreviously unseen input data. Validation datasets are used as feedbackto fine-tune the model accuracy, and the model is tested after validated.A ML model is deemed underfit if it shows high errors on the trainingand validation datasets.

content: model accuracy, and the model is tested after validated.A ML model is deemed underfit if it shows high errors on the trainingand validation datasets. A model is deemed overfit if it accuratelycaptures the training data but has large errors on test dataset. Neitherscenario is ideal, as the model is prone to making errors in predictionsin both cases. However, while underfitting can be present in hypothesisdriven models, overfitting is usually peculiar to data-driven models.Thus, the goal of ML is to obtain a model that is neither overfitor underfit and generalizes well. For further information on modeltraining, bias, and variance, please refer to other resources [20–23]. 2.1.1. Artificial intelligence, ML, and deep learningML, artificial intelligence, and deep learning often cause confusion,particularly in how they are interrelated. Artificial intelligence is theability of a machine to replicate human intelligence in a specific task,thereby allowing it to predict an output. In an ideal situation, bothmachine and human would provide the same output response andjustify it [24].Problem solving capabilities and the ability to learn are the keyelements of artificial intelligence.

content: vide the same output response andjustify it [24].Problem solving capabilities and the ability to learn are the keyelements of artificial intelligence. ML is consequently a subset of artificial intelligence, wherein a model learns from the available data.Tom Mitchell defined ML as a program that could learn a specific taskT through experiences E, thereby enhancing the performance P [25].For example, experience E could be the measured data points in amembrane process (e.g., rejection or permeance), the tasks T are the socalled features (pressure, temperature, flow rate), and the performancemeasure P is the difference between the measured and the ML predictedrejection values (see Fig. 3).Deep learning (DL) is a subset of ML. It uses computational modelsdeveloped for representation learning that comprise many processinglayers to derive complex patterns about the data. The layers of a deeplearning model form a hierarchy, starting with a first layer that capturescertain trends and performs input data featurization. The informationis then transferred to the subsequent layers, which identify patternsin the abstract data. For instance, in image classification, the first fewlayers may detect the edges of an image, whereas the subsequent layerscan identify more intricate features. These learning processes havebeen used in state-of-the-art applications, including and not limited to,speech comprehension and object detection in images [16]. DL will befurther discussed in Section 2.4. 2.2.

Cheminformatics is widely used for pharmaceutical drug discovery [26] and chemical data storage [27]. However, different featurizations and learning algorithms can be used that operateon chemical data [28].The chemical space is a hypothetical space comprising existing andvirtual substances. It is estimated to be 1060 for organic moleculesbelow 500 Da [29]. It is also hyperdimensional; therefore, humanshave difficulty in correlating chemical structure with their respective properties. However, this issue can be efficiently addressed bylearning algorithms. A chemical space is constructed in a way thatsimilar chemical objects remain closer if they share similar properties, which is known as chemical similarity, and considerably impactsthe operation of ML algorithms on molecules and chemical objects.If two chemical objects such as molecules, substances, particles, orradicals have high structural similarity, they will also have similarphysicochemical properties. Thus, this similarity must be measuredusing a distance function in a subspace of the chemical space [30,31].

citation.title: Machine learning for the advancement of membrane science and technology: A critical review

content: o have similarphysicochemical properties. Thus, this similarity must be measuredusing a distance function in a subspace of the chemical space [30,31]. Therefore, the property of a new molecule can be estimatedbased on the properties of a set of molecules, such as its relativesimilarity (distance) to the original set of molecules (Fig. 4a). Thisprocess is known quantitative structure–property relationship (QSPR)for molecule-level estimation or quantitative structure–activity relationship (QSAR) for substance-level estimation. For instance, trendscan be created to estimate toxicity (molecule-level property) or predictboiling point (substance-level property).The methods used to computationally represent such chemical entities is posed with many challenges. High level of abstractions, such as names, CAS numbers, or simplified molecular-input line-entry system (SMILES) does not contain sufficient information for preciselyestimating properties. Therefore, the underlying low-level atomic andchemical properties must be used for each molecules. This process isknown as featurization, wherein an original chemical object such as amolecule, substance, and reaction is injected with domain knowledgeand transformed into another abstraction that can be directly usedby ML algorithms.

content: molecule, substance, and reaction is injected with domain knowledgeand transformed into another abstraction that can be directly usedby ML algorithms. Such a transformation involves the conversion oforganic molecules into a fixed-length chemical fingerprint vectors [32].Then, distance-based and similarity estimation can be performed onthe chemical space spanned by these fingerprint vectors [33]. Anotherexample is when the properties (molecular weight, logP, refractivity,etc.) are calculated and used for similarity estimation [34]. Groupcontribution methods can also be used for QSAR estimations using theproperties of atoms and functional groups [35]. In group-contributiontheory, a relationship is estimated for each atom and functional group.The properties of new molecules can be estimated using the predetermined features of the properties of these functional groups. The majoradvantage of group contribution is that extremely small datasets (<100)can be effectively used [36]. Fingerprints, molecular descriptors, andgroup contributions are generally used with traditional ML algorithmsbecause they can be tabulated (Fig. 4a). For example, fingerprints wereused to build ML algorithms to predict the permeability of gas separation membranes [12]. However, in case of many similar molecules,the fingerprints can lead to bit-collision. Tejus et al. used MACCSkeys to resolve bit-collision which were a key challenge in generativeinverse design applications [13].

The main difference betweengraph-based methods and fingerprints and group contribution is thatthe former does not require transforming a molecule into a higher levelabstraction, but graph-representation can be directly used (Fig. 4b).Thus, information loss and bit-collision is inherently absent for graphbased model. The current, best-performing learning algorithms forchemistry applications are all based on graphs [40]. 2.2.1. Featurization in membrane applicationsA typical membrane separation process comprises three major parts:a matrix, membrane, and process parameters. A matrix contains amixture of particles that a membrane is selective of. Process is a clusterof all process parameters such as temperature, flowrate, pressure, andhumidity. These components in a membrane separation are referredto as the system level (Fig. 5). If we want to represent the wholemembrane separation process for ML applications, an appropriate featurization technique must be found for all three parts. This membranesystem featurization is summarized herein. The matrix, membrane andprocess components can be determined based on membrane applicationsuch as reverse osmosis (RO), gas separation, and microfiltration.

citation.title: Machine learning for the advancement of membrane science and technology: A critical review

content: trix, membrane andprocess components can be determined based on membrane applicationsuch as reverse osmosis (RO), gas separation, and microfiltration. Thesecomponents can be further categorized into types, such as solids parti- cles, gases, active layers, and process parameters, and predeterminedbased on membrane applications. However, in some cases, domainknowledge supersedes the predetermination of these components. Forinstance, if the support layer is known to not affect fouling, then thefeatures of the support layer can be excluded. Based on the componenttypes, parameters can be determined via calculations or measurements,which will be input into the ML algorithm. Fingerprints are a specialparameter clusters, which can be directly generated from molecularstructures. Therefore, they can be used to describe the membrane andmatrix components. A priori selection of parameters is known as manualfeature engineering: a domain knowledge expert includes or excludesfeatures.In practice, the parameters to be input as features into the MLalgorithm can be chosen irrespective of the application requirements,mostly based on domain knowledge. For instance, experimental setupparameters are considered the simplest features, such as concentrationand temperature [11,41–43]. Such simple parameters are useful whenthe features affecting the membrane system are already known. Theseexperimental features are also useful to build fast and responsiveML-based control systems.

content: ures affecting the membrane system are already known. Theseexperimental features are also useful to build fast and responsiveML-based control systems. However, the lack of molecular structuralinformation renders these experimental feature challenging to use instructure–property applications. Feature engineering of molecular interactions, such as HSPs [44] or physicochemical properties [39,45–47], are also common. More sophisticated featurizations include the injection of molecular structural data (solutes, membranes, and solvents)such as circular or Morgan fingerprints [10,48,49]. Fingerprint-basedfeaturization methods are widely used in the literature due to theirsimplicity and their seamless integration with boosting algorithms, thatcan operate well on smaller datasets. Because chemical molecular structures can be mathematically represented as graphs, graph featurizationtechniques are also widely popular. Graph-based models do not involveexplicit featurization of chemical structures; the network learns directlyfrom the graph data [39,50] The internal featurization of GNNs areusually involve pre-defined low-level atomic and bond features, such asatomic number, aromaticity, hybridizations etc. However, sophisticatedGNN algorithms allow additional atom and bond features as well as theconcatenation of molecular-level features [51]. content: 2.3. Traditional models Once a suitable representation of the data has been chosen for thecontext of the problem, and sensible similarity/distance metrics areknown, the next challenge presents itself in model choice. Dependingon the type of problem, one must first choose the type of machine learning needed for the task. A brief overview of the different types can beseen in Fig. 6. In some cases, the goal may be to isolate certain instancesor samples into self-similar clusters, such as Magdăuet al. did forLi+ solvation environments in polymer electrolytes [52]. Often times,the insight from unsupervised learning can be sufficient to informfurther experiments or suggest a path forward. For example, principalcomponent analysis can be used to reveal the features most responsiblefor points being close together in the lower-dimensional manifold bymeans of component loadings. In cases where a direct prediction needsto be made, and when labeled data are available for training, supervisedlearning is the right tool for the job. Supervised learning falls into twomajor subcategories: classification and regression. The former is idealfor situations in which the desired prediction is a category or otherdiscrete variable. If the desired output is a continuous value, regressionmodels are needed.

citation.title: Machine learning for the advancement of membrane science and technology: A critical review

content: ons in which the desired prediction is a category or otherdiscrete variable. If the desired output is a continuous value, regressionmodels are needed. This section will focus on supervised learning,as this field involves slightly more user choice in the construction,training, and evaluation of the models.Once the type of model is chosen, a few more decisions must bemade before getting started. Given the modern prevalence of deeplearning, one may be tempted to try artificial neural networks and othersuch approaches in an attempt to reach the promised accuracy metricsfrom certain headlines. However, further considerations into the natureof the problem might disincentivize deep learning. Deep learning relieson the optimization of a multitude of parameters, which typicallymakes them data-hungry, computationally-costly models. Shallow models need to optimize fewer parameters, making them better suited toscarce data applications, as well as easier to explore after training.The required interpretability of the final model is growing increasinglyimportant in modern applications. Certain machine learning modelscan notoriously fall under the label of ‘‘black boxes’’, where the innerworkings are hidden or otherwise too cumbersome to be handled byusers. In situations where the user needs to understand why a certainprediction is being made, shallow/simple models may have the advantage, as they are much easier to audit [53].

When data fall somewherebetween ‘‘simple’’ and ‘‘complex’’, shallow models can be combinedwith first principles to boost model predictive power as well [54],though this may require some additional feature engineering. For example, in a simple membrane separationscenario, domain knowledge derived features could be temperature,pressure, flowrate and membrane type, but not the geological locationof the experiment. The bare minimum requirement for linear regressionis 2 samples per output, intuitively, but more data is always helpful. Insituations where the number of features greatly exceeds the numberof samples, the system will likely be underspecified, as not enoughequations will be available to solve for the parameters. General guidelines in the machine learning community tend toward 5–10 samples pervariable, and the use of features likely to contain the most descriptivepower, such as pertinent experimental conditions or variables fromknown scientific laws.

To get Bayesian linear regression, the key modification is to estimate the parameters by Bayes’ theorem. Using someprior distribution for the parameters and multiplying by the likelihoodfunction, the result is a posterior distribution of parameter values. Themath behind this can get lengthy, but there are walkthroughs availablein the literature [64]. More common in modern research [9,10] is the concept of Bayesianoptimization. As studies turn to more complex systems, optimizingseveral parameters jointly can become unmanageable by traditionalmeans fairly quickly. The idea behind Bayesian optimization is to treatthe data as noisy observations of some black box function that has noclosed-form solution, then to select ‘‘ideal’’ points to sample next basedon current knowledge of the system. Generally, the approach followsthe equation below: 𝑃(𝒘|𝐷) = 𝑝(𝐷|𝒘)𝑝(𝒘) 𝑝(𝐷)(7) where 𝒘represents the parameters of the model and 𝐷the availabledata. This process begins with a prior distribution over all possibleobjective functions, which is updated to yield posterior distributionsreflecting the new data from query points and the understanding thatcomes with them. At every iteration, an acquisition function is chosento select the next query point based on the current posterior distribution.

This function can take several forms depending on the userneeds, allowing for a balance between exploitation and explorationin the variable space. Once the next input is chosen, the objectivefunction is queried and the surrogate model of the objective function isupdated. Though there are plenty of options for a Bayesian optimization setup, a common one implements a Gaussian process regressionmodel and radial basis function or Matérn kernel [65]. Huang et al.demonstrated Bayesian optimization for the selection of optimal experimental conditions in developing polyvinyl chloride-based ion-selectiveelectrodes [66]. Bayesian methods can also be applied in other ways,such as Chen et al. did in their Monte Carlo-based sampling of polymerchemistry space to identify membranes exceeding the Robeson upperbound [14]. 2.3.4. Boosting methods Boosting is a technique developed to combine several weak learnersinto a single strong learner. It falls under the umbrella of ensemblemethods, which generally covers combinations of multiple models toyield a prediction. Doing so makes the model output more resilient tooutliers or noise in data, as the impact on a few learners is mitigatedby the rest of the ensemble. The tradeoff lies in the tendency ofboosted models to be more computationally expensive.

content: ct on a few learners is mitigatedby the rest of the ensemble. The tradeoff lies in the tendency ofboosted models to be more computationally expensive. The other sideof ensemble methods is referred to as ‘‘bagging’’ algorithms. Boosting methods focus on an initial estimator and subsequent correctionestimators, which reweight the areas on which the model performspoorly, leading to steady improvement. Bagging methods, however,focus on developing several models in parallel on different randomlysubsampled sections of the dataset, such as in a random forest. Thesemethods tend to rely on bootstrapping to accomplish this subsampling.In both cases, the outputs of the weak learners are summed, averaged,or the majority vote is taken for the final ensemble output. Below, wecover a few common approaches for boosting in machine learning [67]. AdaBoost: One of the earliest boosting methods, AdaBoost usesa base learner and subsequently weights each training instance byits own performance. For every iteration of the training process, theinstances over which the model performs the worst are emphasizedthe most, so as to cover the gaps in the training. In other words, theweights vary inversely with model performance on a given trainingexample. The exact method of calculating the weights depends on thespecific variant of AdaBoost chosen. For practical examples, Alanaziet al. implemented AdaBoost with several base learners for their study,previously mentioned in the kNN section [61].

content: Gradient Boosting Machines: For gradient boosting machines, aparticular loss function must be chosen. The main mechanism here is tochoose weights so as to minimize the loss function by gradient descent.The loss function can take several forms, but it must make sense in thecontext of the problem. Improvements to both speed and accuracy werefound in switching to stochastic gradient boosting. LightGBM: LightGBM is a version of a gradient boosting machinethat incorporates a few extra steps to reduce data dimensionality andincrease speed. It uses gradient-based one-side sampling to strategicallyretain samples with higher loss function gradients, as these are thesamples the model moves away from the quickest. In addition to theretention of these high-gradient samples, the rest of the training at agiven iteration is filled with randomly sampled instances from the restof the training set [67]. XGBoost: XGBoost stands for extreme gradient boosting, whichincorporates regularization to mitigate overfitting, subsampling, out-ofcore computation, and split functions capable of recognizing sparsityin available data. The splitting criteria for the trees are also designedto scale well for larger datasets. XGBoost can be seen in the works ofLu et al. in modeling nanofiltration membrane ion selectivity [46] andJeong et al. in predicting micropollutant removal in reverse osmosisand nanofiltration membranes [68].

CatBoost: CatBoost is built around categorical features, but ratherthan handling the transformation to numerical values during preprocessing, it does so during tree splitting. Categorical features are transformed using one-hot encoding if there are relatively few categories,or the average target value for many categories. It also leverages anordered boosting process to avoid overfitting, and can handle missingvalues similar to XGBoost [67]. Ibrar et al. [69] successfully employed CatBoost to predict reverse solute flux in forward osmosis, andYang et al. [70] used CatBoost to predict pervaporation separationfactors based on polymer properties, membrane morphology, and otherexperimental parameters. 2.4. Deep learning methods Over the last decade, DL has emerged as a transformative technology in tackling complex challenges [3,16,71,72], particularly incomputer vision and natural language processing. It has achieved remarkable success across several applications. More recently, DL hasbeen used in chemistry and biology and shown great promise. Forinstance, DL has made significant progress in advancing property prediction in quantum chemistry [73] and accurately predicting proteinstructures [74], demonstrating its broad and potential impact acrossvarious disciplines, including membrane science [39,50].

This sectionoverviews DL methods, highlighting their key advantages and theirrelevance to membrane science and applications. 2.4.1. Artificial neural network A fundamental concept in DL is the artificial neural network (ANN).An ANN is composed of interconnected layers, each containing neurons(or nodes) (Fig. 7a). Apart from the input layer, neurons in one layerestablish weighted connections with all neurons in the preceding layer.Similar to neurons in the human brain, neurons in an ANN transfersignals to the next layer when activated. This activation, influencedby the weights of connections and input data, plays a critical role indetermining the contribution of each neuron to the network output.The essence of DL lies in optimizing these weights to enhance thenetwork accuracy in predicting outcomes such as the energy of molecular structures. ANN design forms the foundational framework for DLarchitectures, empowering them to interpret complex patterns withindata. Deep versus shallow neural network. An ANN has three maincomponents: the input layer, which receives the data; one or morehidden layers, which process the data; and the output layer, whichproduces the final prediction or classification. Graph neural networksGNNs are a subclass of neural networks designed for processinggraph-structured data. A graph contains nodes interconnected by edges(or links) that describe the relation among them. Graphs can describeinteractions of data across a broad spectrum of domains such as molecular structures, protein–protein interactions, knowledge graphs, andsocial networks [91]. For instance, within molecular graphs, an atomand a bond are represented as a node and an edge, respectively. Themain mechanism that empowers GNNs in processing graph data iscalled message passing.The foundation for message passing in GNNs was initially introduced in a study [37,92–95] that explored the early formulationsof this concept.

citation.title: Machine learning for the advancement of membrane science and technology: A critical review

content: passing.The foundation for message passing in GNNs was initially introduced in a study [37,92–95] that explored the early formulationsof this concept. This idea was refined and systematically formulatedinto the Message Passing Neural Networks (MPNN) framework [96].While initially proposed for quantum chemistry applications, the MPNNframework has become a cornerstone in the development of GNNsacross a wide array of applications. In the MPNN framework, messagepassing operates by an exchange of information between each nodeand its neighbors. Specifically, during a single message passing layer,each node sends a message to its neighbors and simultaneously receivesmessages from them. These incoming messages are aggregated to update the state of the receiving node. Using this process, enriched nodefeatures that capture the local structural representation of the graph canbe constructed, thus enabling the model to make informed predictions.We show a simple demonstration of message passing in Fig. 7b.SchNet [73] is one of the significant advancements in messagepassing networks for predicting quantum chemical properties, suchas energy and forces. It uses the distance between atoms as edgefeatures during training. The key advantage of using distances is theirinvariance to translation and rotation. When a 3D molecular structureis rotated, the distances between atoms remain constant although the3D coordinates of atoms may change.

content: 2.4.3. Pre-training and transfer learningIn the past, GNN models were often trained with randomly initialized network parameters. However, recently, there has been a growing trend toward pre-training, especially on large-scale datasets. Pretraining on large-scale datasets enables the extraction of generalizedfeatures that can be applied across various prediction tasks. Largescale pre-trained models, often referred to as foundation models, havetransformed image recognition and natural language processing [103–107]. A key to this success is the availability of large amounts ofdata. Although several molecular datasets exist, such as QM9 [108]and MD17 [109], they typically range in size from thousands to afew million instances. However, models in the aforementioned domainsare pre-trained on considerably large datasets such as JFT-300M [110](containing 300 million images) and LAION-5B (containing 5.85 billionimage-text pairs) [111].

content: Molecular datasets, such as OC20 [112], OC22 [113], and OpenDAC [114], have recently been introduced, containing tens to hundreds of millions density functional theory (DFT) calculations. This hasopened new opportunities in the field of chemistry to apply knowledgefrom models pre-trained on these large-scale datasets to specializedprediction tasks [115,116]. The introduction of JMP framework [8]further enhances the capability of pre-training and transfer learningin chemistry by supporting joint pre-training on multiple datasetsfollowed by fine-tuning on diverse prediction tasks.At present, no examples have been reported in membrane literaturewhere models are pre-trained or fine-tuned on small datasets. However, fine-tuning large foundation models on specific membrane-relatedapplications might be inevitable in the near future, as both wet-laband simulated membrane data are laborand resource-intensive. Weanticipate that fine-tuning will lead to a surge in specialized predictivemodels, particularly in areas with data scarcity. 2.5. Generative models in membrane design Generative tasks involve developing models that can produce samples that mimic the distribution of a given dataset. For example,consider a dataset that contains the water permeance of TFC membranes using a monomer structure [117] A generative model trainedon this dataset learns to represent its distribution.

Consequently, whenpresented with a desired permeance input, the model can generateplausible monomers to achieve this. Once trained, generative modelscan generate new samples that resemble the training data by samplingfrom the learned distribution. Generative adversarial networks (GANs)are commonly used for this task[118]. In GANs, a generator and adiscriminator play a competitive game where the generator attemptsto create samples that can fool the discriminator, and the discriminatorlearns to become better at identifying the generated samples.While GANs are popular generative models, several other techniques have emerged in recent years such as diffusion models that learnto map samples to a latent space by gradually adding noise to the data.Diffusion-based techniques have been key to the success of state-of-theart models such as AlphaFold 3 [119], which has achieved impressiveresults compared with previously reported methods in predicting thestructure of biomolecular interactions. Although generative models are promising forapplications in membrane science and other fields, some key limitationsneed to be carefully considered [120,122]. One major challenge withsuch models is the requirement of large datasets. Generative modelstypically depend on extensive datasets to effectively capture the complex distributions of real-world data. Training on low-data regimes maygenerate low-quality or unrealistic samples. Research areas such asfew-shot learning, which seeks to train models on a small number ofexamples, are being explored to mitigate this issue in low-data regimes[123]. Moreover, these models may not appropriately handle out-ofdistribution inputs. Given that generative models aim to mimic thetraining data distribution, they are inherently limited in their capacityto generalize to new, unseen distributions. When presented with inputsthat are significantly deviated from the training data, the modelsmay struggle to generate meaningful outputs. Furthermore, complexarchitectures and high-dimensional latent spaces used by models suchas GANs and diffusion models often make it difficult to interpretand explain the factors that influence the generation process. Thisposes challenges in understanding the reasoning behind a particulargenerated output. 2.6.

Importance of data in ML Data is the most important ingredient for a successful machinelearning model, generally having a disproportionately large impact onperformance relative to other factors (such as the specific trainingprocedure used or choice of model architecture from sensible candidates) [124]. The majority of machine learning discussion outsideof the scientific community (such as news articles) focuses on thedevelopment of new machine learning models, such as the relativelyrecent development of the transformer, the meteoric rise of largelanguage models and general developments in the technology sector.This is largely because the discussion of the ‘‘AI’’ itself is simply moreexciting than the discussion of the data used to train the models.Unfortunately, this can create the false impression that the model iscapable of compensating for poor data practices. In this section, wediscuss the best practices in collecting, curating, and processing data formachine learning. In addition, we will review, from a model-agnosticperspective, the ways in which data can impact machine learning modelperformance. We also highlight two excellent libraries for handlingdata and performing data processing: Pandas [125–127] and Sci-kitLearn [128]. 2.6.1.

Choosing your data Every scientific problem should start with a testable hypothesis, andfor machine learning-driven research, once a problem is identified, thenext step is choosing an appropriate dataset. For an arbitrary machinelearning application, a dataset must not only be large enough to providesufficient training examples for the model, it must be appropriatelyrelevant to the scientific use case. The choice of dataset is criticaland must be carefully considered through the lens of the problem athand [129]. We begin with a discussion of relevance. A large dataset is notnecessarily a relevant one, and a relevant dataset might not necessarilybe large (or need to be large). The key factor in determining which datato use is your deployment scenario. In other words, for best performance,the data you use to train your model should be in-distribution withrespect to the data you want to deploy your model on. To put thisinto perspective, if the deployment scenario is reverse osmosis and thecollected data is below 10 bars only, predicting at 100 bars is out ofthe training distribution. However, determining whether or not trainingand testing data are in-distribution with respect to each other is notnecessarily trivial when dealing with complex, scientific datasets.

content: not trainingand testing data are in-distribution with respect to each other is notnecessarily trivial when dealing with complex, scientific datasets. However, there are tricks and best practices which researchers can use to help make this determination. For example, by using chemical intuition(e.g. a database of largely aromatic compounds is likely out-of-samplewith respect to a database of largely aliphatic compounds) [130] orvia more heuristic methods (e.g. sampling from two distributions andchecking whether not they are visually distinguishable) [131]. The size of the dataset is also important to account for. In particular,the size of the available data can help inform both the choice ofmodel and featurization method (discussed in the next section). Asmaller dataset necessitates simpler models, such as Gaussian processes,random forests or polynomial fits, with less parameters so as to prevent over-fitting. This also results in increased model interpretability.Manual featurization is recommended in these cases, since data-driventechniques for featurizing small datasets can succumb to the sameoverfitting problems as training the models themselves. Larger datasetscan make use of more complex models with higher information capacity, such as neural networks, and can leverage more sophisticatedfeaturization techniques, including the use of deep learning, whichitself can be thought of as a data-driven featurization method. 2.6.2.

Data cleaning and featurization Once a dataset is identified, it must be cleaned and featurized.Data cleaning is a process whereby the data are screened for errors toensure that they are free from inconsistencies for use in the model. Forthe reader interested in technical details, we recommend the excellentreview by García et al. [132]. Some methods of data cleaning includede-duplication, outlier removal, feature imputation (the process ofinferring missing values in the dataset from other entries) [133], andde-noising [134]. This step is important for multiple reasons, namelythat machine learning models (generally) require no missing fields, anddepending on the choice of model, can be sensitive to outliers. Manual or at least some form of non-trivial featurization is oftenrequired when dealing with scientific data due to the unique challengesit presents. Often, each training example is high-dimensional (has manydegrees of freedom), and usually dataset size is limited (relative toe.g. the datasets available in the tech sector). Featurization can take many forms and is especially important inthe field of membrane science, which generally deals with molecularinputs.

content: ch sector). Featurization can take many forms and is especially important inthe field of membrane science, which generally deals with molecularinputs. In machine learning, inputs and outputs have to be numeric, andsome notion of distance must exist, such that two examples with similarfeatures (as measured by that distance) are indeed similar [131].This begs the question: how does one define a distance between twomolecules or polymers? Multiple methods exist, depending on the typeand amount of data available. If significant data are available, datadriven methods, such as processing natural language-based encodingsof molecules e.g. SMILES directly can be effective. Molecules canalso be compressed into latent representations which can be used asfeatures [135]. If minimal data are available one can convert moleculesor polymers into a hashed representation [136] or fingerprint, whichvary in terms of information capacity and interpretability [13]. 2.6.3. Too much or too little data? As previously mentioned, the data directly determine the qualityof the model. Ideally, a smaller dataset requires a simpler model asopposed to a much larger dataset, which tends to require a morecomplex model. When the model learns the patterns in the data toowell, often due to having too many trainable parameters or insufficientregularization, and does not respond effectively with unseen data, ithas been overfit.

content: ften due to having too many trainable parameters or insufficientregularization, and does not respond effectively with unseen data, ithas been overfit. In contrast, when the model is too simple (too few parameters) or regularization has been overtuned, the model is unable tocapture the finer patterns in the data and will not be able to accuratelypredict the output. In this situation, it has been underfit [137]. Ultimately, there is no ‘correct’ amount of data. It is a trade offbetween the size of the dataset and the complexity of the model. The main issue which needs improving within the membrane field isthat many articles do not share all the data which can result in accuracyissues. For example, properties that affect membrane performance suchas porosity and pore volume are characteristics that are not usuallyshared [138]. Therefore, there should be more knowledge about openaccess data to encourage the sharing of free and accessible content. Journal of Membrane Science 713 (2025) 123256

Using different trajectories of MD simulation,the mechanical properties and transport properties of a large systemsuch as biomolecules and synthetic membranes can be predicted. 2.7.1. Quantum mechanical methods in membrane scienceDFT is used for quantum mechanical calculation to describe theelectronic structures of atoms and molecules. DFT approximates theelectron correlations using electron density functionals, whereas abinito methods compute the electronic structure directly from physicallaws. DFT calculations are generally faster due to electron density approximations. Therefore, large electronic systems (up to 100 atoms) canbe rapidly optimized using DFT than ab initio methods such as Hartree–Fock. In DFT, the total energy of the system is derived from the electronic density of molecules and external forces. Membrane propertiesand interaction, such as hydrogen-bonding, and London dispersion noncovalent interactions can be studied via DFT calculations [154]. Kimet al. investigated the interaction of polyethersulfone and polysulfonewith different ionic liquids via DFT calculations for membrane preparation. Results showed that the polyethersulfone–ionic liquid systemwith higher interaction energies were suitable for membrane preparation than polysulfone–ionic liquid system with lower interactionenergies. Their solubility results correlated well with experimentalobservations [155].

Integration of machine learning with MD simulation ML techniques have been used to speed up or replace traditionalDFT and MD approaches. To overcome slow calculation time – one ofthe main challenges with force fields in MD simulation – ML-based forcefields such as DCF, SchNet, GNNFF, TorchMD, ACEMD3, CGSchNet,ANI and np2p NNP have been developed to replace QM-based parameters. These models work by estimating/modeling interatomic potentialusing ML techniques. ML models usually have a faster inference timethan ab initio and DFT calculations; however, they usually cannotreach sufficiently high precision. Therefore, hybrid ML–MD simulationscan offer fast and precise calculations [167,168]. Computational andexperimental data on polymeric and 2D membranes have been obtainedin various studies, and ML tools have been to predict membraneswith anticipated properties [9,13,169]. Using these tools, a wide rangeof materials can be screened and optimized and the most promisingtool can be selected to reduce time required for computations. [9,10,13,169,170]. Predictive approaches Several studies have attempted to develop membranes with desirable attributes such as high solvent permeability and high soluteselectivity [41,182]. Studies to datehave only focused on enhancing parameters such as the quality ofpermeate (fresh water), tackling water contamination, and membraneperformance parameters such as fouling [171]. To further develop uponthis work, other parameters such as detecting the thickness of thefouling layer could be further investigated. By immediately relayingthe thickness relayed to a computer, the feed composition can beautomatically changed to prevent further damage to membranes.

content: 3.1.3. Organic Solvent Nanofiltration (OSN) and Organic Solvent ReverseOsmosis (OSRO) Organic solvent nanofiltration (OSN) or solvent-resistant nanofiltration (SRNF) is a pressure-driven liquid–liquid separation technologyperformed in organic solvent applications. OSN membranes usually operate between organic solvent reverse osmosis (OSRO) and ultrafiltration membranes, where size and physicochemical parameters influencethe solute permeation through the membrane. Similar to OSRO, thehigh-dimensionality of solute–solvent–membrane interaction renderstraditional analytical approaches challenging to solve [234]. The use of computer aided design in OSN membranes was firstintroduced by Vankelecom et al. [235] where they optimized the composition of the membrane using genetic algorithm in order to improvethe performance of the membrane. Similarly, simulations in OSN wereevaluated by Peshev et al. [236] to simulate OSN processes using theconcept of an ‘OSN Designer’. Schmidt et al. [237] proposed processintegration and introduced membrane modeling maps. Studies in OSN have slowly deviated toward using computationalmodels to enhance membrane properties such as solute rejection [238]and solvent flux. However, studies on improving the material or composition of membranes have been rarely reported. Goebel et al. [239] introduced a novel hybrid ML model in OSN using genetic programming along with global deterministic optimization that determinedoptimal parameters.

Such a model will be helpfulfor membrane suppliers to screen membranes in specific applicationswith high solvent permeance. In a follow-up study, Goebel et al. [240]predicted solute rejection in a solvent mixture, instead of flux, forPuraMem S600 membranes using ML models. They used the sameconcept of genetic programming, which proved to be accurate as thedifference between actual and predicted rejections had a less than 10%deviation. Membrane rejection maps were generated based on puresolvents and solvent mixtures without conducting experiments. Thesemaps could be effectively used as tools for identifying the rejectionof specific membranes in OSN processes. The majority of studies onOSN focuses on investigating the permeate flux and solute rejections,and data-driven augmentation of transport mechanisms remain widelyunexplored, which can be a potential area of future research [42]. Hu et al. [11] used a range of ML algorithms (ANN, RF, andSVM) to predict membrane performance in OSN based on rejectionand permeance using collected data points.

content: 3.1.6. Fuel cellsMembrane fuel cells and in particular proton exchange membranefuel cells (PEMFCs) have become a popular topic of conversationfollowing an emerging hydrogen economy. These membranes exhibithighly desirable properties, such as high proton flux and stability [76,270]. Other types of fuel cells include solid oxide fuel cells, alkaline [271] and anion-exchange membrane fuel cells [272]. Fuel cellsare particularly attractive for transportation companies [271]. Challenges that arise within the field of using fuel cells is the durabilityperformance as well as the cost. Hence, most research tends to focus on optimizing these features, with a specific focus on optimizingthe design, material configuration as well as operational parameters(proton conductivity and water uptake [273]) in PEMFCs. The useof ANNs have been popular within PEMFCs and explored by manyresearchers [274,275] whereby parameters such as the power of thecells were modeled. Decision tree algorithms have also been extensivelyresearched in fuel cell applications [276].Wang et al. [76] used DL to analyze and evaluate the transportof water on a microscale, allowing for the continued improvementof fuel cell design. Interestingly, Rui et al.

The approach helped in designinghigh performance fuel cell samples and reducing time and costs in thelab. Another major set back in fuel cell technology is the high cost ofcatalysts in the electrode. To combat this issue, ML has been integratedto further improve the cost of MEA in PEMFCs [87]. Ding et al. content: 3.1.7. Membranes as porous materialsPorous membranes have defined pores in their structure. The sizeand distribution of these pores are used to categorize membranes andtheir potential applications, such as ultra-, microor particle (sediment)filtration [212]. Notable examples include zeolite membranes [283],ceramic membranes [284], covalent organic frameworks (COFs) [172],fine-tuned zeolitic-imidazolate frameworks (ZIFs) [285] or membranesderived from MOFs [174,286–288]. The performance of a membrane ishighly dependent on its morphology and geometry [58]. Features suchas the porosity [289], fractional free volume [170] and the pore sizedistribution [290] can highly influence the membranes selectivity andpermeability feature. All of these mentioned features are also importantfor general (non-membrane related) porous materials as well. Therefore, non-membrane related studies could offer valuable insight forpotential membrane applications [291]. Hence, many researchers havecoupled the use of computational resources to enhance these features tosave time from conducting experimental measures in the laboratory forboth membrane and other non-membrane related applications [292].The correlation between the morphological features to their permeability of any materials is long-standing challenge. content: Fig. 16. Generative artificial intelligence for membrane technology applications. Material, membrane, or process designs can be obtained through direct or inverse design toobtain a desired target property or multiple properties. Direct design includes the traditional trial-and-error approach, along with direct molecular simulations based on a prioriconceptualization. Inverse design harnesses data-driven approaches, either through high-throughput screening and virtual search, optimization on the design space, or trainedgenerative design using models such as generative adversarial networks, variational autoencoders, or transformers. Example applications for each of the inverse design approachesis listed from the literature on membrane science or related fields. In particular, the study of MOF’s integrated with physics basedmodeling has been popular. The prediction of the adsorption properties of individual gasses and their mixtures [294] such as krypton and xenon [295], hydrogen separations [176,296] and carbondioxide applications [297–299] have been the subject to extensiveresearch. Overall, these studies use an extensive MOF dataset withMD calculated features, such as volumetric surface area, pore limitingdiameters and largest cavity diameters to derive structure–propertyrelationships. Later, the models are used to explore hypothetical MOFsin virtual search inverse design approach. For example Peng et al.

Additionally, resistance to fouling as well asstability and durability of membranes should be studied to understandtheir life expectancy, eventually introducing data-driven concepts toreduce operational and capital costs. This gap shouldalso be looked into for the future of membrane technology. 3.2. Generative design approaches In this section, we enumerate the studies conducted on the polymer,membrane, and process scales of generative design using ML techniques. We also provide an outlook on the future of inverse design inthe field of membrane technology. 3.2.1. Polymer design The generative design of novel membranes and membrane separation is inherently dependent on the design of new materials.

citation.title: Machine learning for the advancement of membrane science and technology: A critical review

content: nology. 3.2.1. Polymer design The generative design of novel membranes and membrane separation is inherently dependent on the design of new materials. Therefore,the key to the inverse design of highly efficient membranes lies in theinverse design of custom polymers with fine-tuned solute selectivity.The fundamental paradigm shift in material design, and thereforepolymer design, comprises abandoning the linear research approach infavor of closed-loop design processes with integrated feedback [135,312]. Traditional research approaches span from material conceptualization to experimental characterization and validation, and follow atrial-and-error approach. However, modern computational capabilitiesenable conceptualization using generative design and optimization. Direct approaches to assess the desired properties of a novel material relyon experiments or simulation, whereas inverse design makes deductionsbased on large molecular or material datasets in an adequate chemicalspace. Inverse design can be generally achieved using several differentphilosophies, as shown in Fig. 16. High-throughput virtual screening (HTVS) directly processes the data points in a chemical space,thereby identifying materials with optimal properties. Alternatively,generative ML models can be trained on the material space, enablingtargeted matter or molecular design using variational autoencoders(VAE), generative adversarial networks (GAN), or reinforcement learning (RL).

citation.title: Machine learning for the advancement of membrane science and technology: A critical review

content: nablingtargeted matter or molecular design using variational autoencoders(VAE), generative adversarial networks (GAN), or reinforcement learning (RL). Evolutionary or Bayesian algorithms can also be used toperform optimization on the design space. The de novo design of polymers necessitates the curation of polymerand monomer databases with a sufficiently large number of records,such as the open macromolecular genome (OMG) [313]. Kim et al.proposed combining the OMG database with a VAE-based generativealgorithm with Bayesian feature selection for the computational designof polymers with target properties [314]. Emphasizing the synthesizability of the predicted polymers, they demonstrated 𝑅2 scores above0.90 for the logP values of the proposed polymers. Further polymerdatabases for materials design include PoLyInfo [315], and the PI1Mbenchmark database for polymer-related cheminformatics [316]. Kim et al. used GA to optimize the design of novel polymers withdedicated properties [317]. The evolutionary optimization approachwas coupled with Gaussian process-based property prediction to produce new and chemically distinct polymers with higher glass transitiontemperatures and large bandgaps. After 100 evolutionary generations,132 novel polymers with the desired properties were identified. Another generative approach was presented by Batra et al. who appliedsyntax-directed VAEs to propose novel polymers for high-temperatureand high-electric-field applications [318].

The unsupervised trainingof VAEs was performed on one-hot-encoded parse trees generatedfrom a polymer SMILES. A subsequent supervised learning step was implemented to train Gaussian process regression models on polymerswith known properties. The polyG2G approach proposed by Gurnani et al. employed graphto-graph translation to generate novel polymers, which were thenfiltered by fitness for optimal polymer selection [319]. The graph-tograph algorithm performed small differentiations in polymer chemistryand structures, leading to significant property changes. The model wasapplied to design novel polymer dielectrics, which produced polymerstructures with superior resistance to electric fields at high temperatures. The polyG2G pipeline, however, is limited to generating linearhomopolymers and alternating copolymers only, with ring structuresthat are already included in the training data. Design space exploration for the generation of novel materials canalso be coupled with MD simulations. An inverse design approachpublished by Wang et al. has explored the generation of novel solidpolymer electrolytes using coarse-grained MD (CGMD) and ML [320].The discrete chemical space was transformed into a continuous designspace using coarse-grained simulations, on which exploration was conducted using Bayesian optimization.

The said approach was built on Bayesian molecular design forpredicting the glass transition temperature and melting temperature aswell as backward prediction of suitable structures. The work was complemented with transfer learning for thermal conductivity predictionutilizing ML models pretrained for predicting proxy properties. The growing number of publications on inverse and generative denovo polymer design necessitates the meta analysis and comparativeassessment of different algorithms [322]. Membrane design Although the generative design of polymers with target propertiesis being extensively studied, the inverse design of membranes is still arelatively unexplored area of research. Membranes are dependent onthe structural properties of constituent materials, including ceramicsor polymers. They also possess morphological characteristics on several scales spanning from the micro-level to the macro-level, whichcan considerably vary between membrane specimens. Moreover, keymembrane performance descriptors, such as permeance and rejection,are substantially dependent on their physicochemical interactions withexternal compounds. Several databases have been established that contain rejection andpermeance data related to different membrane technologies [140,242].Consequently, methods for optimizing the chemical space of membraneseparations are being developed. Wang et al. proposed an approach toidentify thin-film composite membranes with enhanced solvent recovery properties [117]. They examined membrane monomers as well asscreened and filtered novel membrane candidates. One membrane wasalso validated through synthetic preparation and testing. Giro et al.

Metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) are often incorporated intomixed matrix membranes to increase the selectivity of the separationsystem. As such, the inverse and optimal design of MOFs can substantially improve the performance of membrane technologies. Zhou et al.combined mechanistic and data-driven modeling for the inverse designof MOFs [296], by performing HTVS on more than 12,000 MOF structures to identify materials with high selectivity in hydrogen–deuteriumgas separation. In addition to physics-based modeling, several MLalgorithms have been used to predict the separation performance ofMOFs. A key step toward inverse design is the identification of MOFfeatures that generally contribute toward better selectivity. The studywas extended to MOF-based ethane/ethene separations, complementing the workflow with a GA-based inverse design [329]. As a result,materials with exceptionally high adsorption selectivity of ethane andhigh membrane selectivity toward ethene were identified. A combination of molecular simulations and ML has also beenapplied for screening MOFs for propane/propylene separation [177].Furthermore, GAs have been used to guide the design of porous materials [195]. Yasuda et al. used evolutionary optimization and MLto propose novel porous materials with enhanced permeability andfiltration efficiency.

The application of GANs in membrane inverse design is limited inthe literature, and the complete inverse design of membrane structures using GANs has not been reported yet. However, a few studieshave harnessed the advantages and power of GANs to develop novelmembranes. Kawada et al. used MD-GAN to accelerate MD simulations for determining the self-diffusion coefficient of water in variouspolymers, including nonlinear copolymers [330]. They successfullypredicted diffusion properties and reported on the importance of usingdomain knowledge on smaller datasets. Auxiliary classifier GANs wereused to model particle deposition on polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF)membranes [331]. This generative model was trained on scanningelectron microscopy (SEM) images of PVDF membrane samples beforeand after water filtration; after filtration, particles were deposited onthe membrane. The generative model successfully reconstructed thedeposition pattern of particles, indicating that it can be a valuable toolfor analyzing fouling in membrane separation.

content: 3.2.3. Process design Generative ML models can be used both on the material leveland the process level of separation technologies. Advancements inprocess design have a fundamental effect on membrane technology, asthe efficient and sustainable implementation of membrane separationsystems is highly dependent on process modeling, intensification, anddigitalization [312]. Generative AI is an emerging research field in chemical and processengineering [332]. Vogel, Balhorn, and Schweidtmann applied a transformer model for the autocompletion of process flowsheets [333], usinga string representation of flowsheet topologies in SFILES 2.0 formatfor model training on a synthetically generated pretraining dataset.The generative model could learn the grammatical rules of the inputlanguage and complement incomplete design patterns. This approachhas also been extended to the generation of control structures [334].Thus, such models and algorithms will become invaluable tools indesigning novel filtration technologies. Although ML has been used in the modeling, optimization, andcontrol of filtration processes, the use of generative AI in the completemultiscale design of membrane separations has not been reported yet.Efforts toward inverse membrane process design include the work ofZhu et al. who developed ML models for membrane capacitive deionization and applied a data-driven approach in desalination performanceprediction and control optimization [335]. The workflow demonstrated the integrationof data driven models with mechanistic process models for improvedaccuracy and efficiency. Ion transport data is initially generated, themodels (ANNs) are trained on the generated data. Integrated intomechanistic process models enabled the simulation and optimizationof the whole membrane plant. Ideally, a challenge exists between assessing the accuracy of a modelversus the efficiency and cost of computational resources used. Insuch situations, experiments can be very accurate as opposed to usingcomputers but tend to be time consuming. On the other hand, certainsimulations tend to be highly accurate but may require a long time tocompute. Realistically, there is no measure to assess which method ismore ideal and each application will need to be considered on a caseby-case basis. [337].Ensuring reproducible results and codes for chemistry application, webase our guideline on the suggestions by Artrith et al. [338] an on aprevalent guideline for QSAR models by Tropsha [339]. The shift toward an open-science ecosystem – incorporating reproducible data, models and workflow – is progressing within thefield computational chemistry of materials [340]. In computationalchemistry, reproducibility and the application of standard methods,such as density functional theory, has already been standardized [341].The same approach is necessary for data-driven methods in membrane science. Machine learning in membrane-related applications isan emerging field where data is crucial, yet protocols and standardsare not well-defined. As with any scientific report, it is crucial toprovide adequate information and data for a machine learning studyto be critically evaluated and replicable.

A set of guidelines have been presented below, which can be considered when developing and implementing ML models. These recommendations should aid creating robust models, ensuring clarity inmanuscripts, and establishing the credibility required for statisticaltools to achieve broad acceptance and utility in the field of chemistry. Data sources f Identify reliable databases (OSN Database, Open MembraneDatabase, PubChem, Materials Project). f Ensure data origin and authenticity. 4.1.2. Identify & select data types f Molecular structures: SMILES, InChI-keys, XYZ coordinates. f Spectral data: NMR, IR or UV–Vis. f Surface image data: Optical, SEM and TEM images. f Crystallographic and protein data: CIF and PDB files. f Experimental properties: boiling points, melting points, solubility. 4.1.3. Data cleaning f Remove duplicates and inconsistencies. Further reading about thenegative effect of data duplication: [343]. f Handle missing values appropriately (imputation or removal). f Normalize or standardize data as needed [344]. f Make sure that the data is representative is true for the intendedfuture application. 4.1.4. Feature engineering f Generate solute, membrane and solvent descriptors: molecularweight, logP, thickness, roughness etc. f Use software tools for descriptor calculation: e.g., RDKit [345],Materials Studio, Mordred [346]. f Or create custom features relevant to the problem domain. 4.2. Select algorithm and model architecture f Select appropriate algorithms (e.g., Boosting algorithms, SVM,Neural Networks). f Consider domain-specific models (e.g., graph neural networks formolecular graphs). f Design neural network architectures suitable for the data type(e.g., ANNs for spectral data, GNNs for molecular graphs). f Select and test multiple algorithms to compare. This helps mitigating overfitting and overdefinition.

4.2.2. Model training f Split data into training, validation, and test sets. f Use cross-validation to ensure robustness [347,348]. f Employ techniques like transfer learning if applicable.

4.2.3. Hyperparameter tuning f Perform grid search or random search for optimal hyperparameters. f Use automated tools for hyperparameter optimization (e.g., Optuna, Hyperopt) [349].

4.3. Model evaluation and validation 4.3.1. Select evaluation metrics f Choose appropriate metrics (e.g., RMSE, MAE, 𝑅2 for regression;receiver operating characteristic (ROC), Matthew’s correlationcoefficient, F1 and accuracy for classification) [350]. f Assess model performance comprehensively.

4.3.2. Validation techniques f Use k-fold cross-validation [347,348]. f Apply external validation on independent datasets.

4.3.3. Prevent overfitting f Implement regularization techniques (L1, L2) [351]. f Use dropout in neural networks [352]. f Monitor learning curves and employ early stopping [353].

4.4. Interpretation and explainability 4.4.1. Model explainability f Use SHAP or LIME for model interpretation [354]. f Generate feature importance plots.

4.4.2. Domain knowledge integration f Incorporate already existing membrane domain knowledge tovalidate the results. f Ensure that the models’ predictions are chemically and physicallyplausible (e.g. negative predicted permeance data is suspicious). Journal of Membrane Science 713 (2025) 123256

4.5.1. Ethical use f Consider the ethical implications of model predictions [355]. f Ensure responsible use of AI in research and applications.

4.6. Documentation and reporting

4.6.1. Comprehensive documentation f Document data sources, preprocessing steps, and feature engineering processes. f Keep detailed records of model development and evaluation.

4.6.2. Transparent reporting f Report results transparently, including limitations and potential biases. f Provide access to code and data for reproducibility.

5. Challenges and future outlook

Data-driven approaches, such as ML and DL, have been used invarious applications of membrane separation. Traditional approachesalong with DNNs can be tools for downstream prediction tasks orguide the physical understanding of high-dimensional problems. Theversatility and broad applicability of ML will eventually become apractical tool in any researchers’ toolbox. Predictive and generativemodels can identify ideal operation conditions or materials to reducetime, labor, cost, or emissions. Current trends suggests that ML will beseamlessly and organically integrated into membrane science. While a plethora of ML models have been reported in the literature for different field, several areas still require improvement.Such examples include datasets and networks fine-tuned for membraneapplication.

5.1. Closing the loop

content:
Generic materials historically reach the market in 15–20 years [356]in a so-called linear design approach. Fig. 18 shows the four steps of thislinear approach for polymeric membranes. The application concept isbased on a human intuition, followed by material synthesis, membranefabrication, as well as analysis and testing. The cycle closes if the newmembrane does not meet the requirements, and the loop continues. Toreduce time, energy, and material costs, closing the loop with an MLintegrated pipeline can accelerate the process. Fig. 18, where all foursteps of the development cycle are governed and monitored by machineintelligence. We envision that fully or partially closing the loop inmembrane development will considerably accelerate the historicallyslow and linear development process.

5.2. Robotics

citation.title: Machine learning for the advancement of membrane science and technology: A critical review

content: The integration of robotic platforms into the continuous feedbackloop is necessary for closed-loop systems. Robotic platforms have beenintegrated into novel material and process development such as photocatalyst synthesis [357], metal oxide synthesis [357] mechanical stressdetermination [358], polymer fabrication [359] or optimal electrolytedevelopment [360]. These automated robotic platforms have higherprecision and accuracy than their human counterparts. We imaginethat membrane material synthesis and development will heavily benefitfrom the integration of automated platforms. However, the challenge ofminiaturization on both fabrication and process testing will still needto be overcome in the future.
